verses.text: Dinah, the only daughter named in Jacob's house, goes out to see the women of the land. She steps outside the family circle — not a sin, just a girl curious about other women's lives. Genesis introduces her by her mother Leah, not by her father.

Shechem, the prince's son, sees her, seizes her, and rapes her. The text does not soften any of this. He saw, he took, he lay with her by force, he humbled her — four verbs of violence in one sentence.

Afterward, his soul clings to Dinah. He speaks kindly to her and asks his father to get me this woman as a wife. The Bible refuses to call assault love, even when the attacker tries to. Affection after violence does not undo the violence.

Afterward, his soul clings to Dinah. He speaks kindly to her and asks his father to get me this woman as a wife. The Bible refuses to call assault love, even when the attacker tries to. Affection after violence does not undo the violence.

Jacob hears what has been done to his daughter. His sons are in the fields with the herds. Jacob holds his peace until they come. The father stays silent. Dinah's brothers will speak before her father will.

Hamor goes out to negotiate. Jacob's sons come in from the field — and when they hear, they are grieved and very angry. Their sister has been treated as folly in Israel, the text says — a thing which ought not to be done. Their fury is rightly named.

Hamor goes out to negotiate. Jacob's sons come in from the field — and when they hear, they are grieved and very angry. Their sister has been treated as folly in Israel, the text says — a thing which ought not to be done. Their fury is rightly named.

Hamor's offer: my son longs for your daughter — give her to him. Intermarry with us. Settle here, trade, possess land. He treats the violence as the start of a business arrangement, never naming what was done.

Hamor's offer: my son longs for your daughter — give her to him. Intermarry with us. Settle here, trade, possess land. He treats the violence as the start of a business arrangement, never naming what was done.

Hamor's offer: my son longs for your daughter — give her to him. Intermarry with us. Settle here, trade, possess land. He treats the violence as the start of a business arrangement, never naming what was done.
Shechem himself adds: let me find grace; whatever dowry, whatever gift you ask, I will give. Only give me the woman. He thinks rape can be paid for. He doesn't know what he has broken.
Shechem himself adds: let me find grace; whatever dowry, whatever gift you ask, I will give. Only give me the woman. He thinks rape can be paid for. He doesn't know what he has broken.

The sons of Jacob answer deceitfully — because he had defiled their sister. We cannot give our sister to an uncircumcised man, they say. That would be a reproach. Lies grow up in the soil of unaddressed harm.

The sons of Jacob answer deceitfully — because he had defiled their sister. We cannot give our sister to an uncircumcised man, they say. That would be a reproach. Lies grow up in the soil of unaddressed harm.

Only on this condition: every male here must be circumcised, like us. Then we will intermarry and become one people. If not, we take our sister and go. They use the covenant sign as a weapon. Holy things misused become deadly.

Only on this condition: every male here must be circumcised, like us. Then we will intermarry and become one people. If not, we take our sister and go. They use the covenant sign as a weapon. Holy things misused become deadly.

Only on this condition: every male here must be circumcised, like us. Then we will intermarry and become one people. If not, we take our sister and go. They use the covenant sign as a weapon. Holy things misused become deadly.

Hamor and Shechem are pleased. Shechem doesn't delay — he had delight in Jacob's daughter. He was more honourable than all his father's house. The Hebrew word for honourable here just means weightier, more prominent. He is the prince's son. It does not mean he is good.

Hamor and Shechem are pleased. Shechem doesn't delay — he had delight in Jacob's daughter. He was more honourable than all his father's house. The Hebrew word for honourable here just means weightier, more prominent. He is the prince's son. It does not mean he is good.

Hamor and Shechem persuade the men at the city gate: these men are peaceful; let them live among us, trade with us, marry with us. The only condition: every male must be circumcised. They sell it as economic opportunity.

Hamor and Shechem persuade the men at the city gate: these men are peaceful; let them live among us, trade with us, marry with us. The only condition: every male must be circumcised. They sell it as economic opportunity.

Hamor and Shechem persuade the men at the city gate: these men are peaceful; let them live among us, trade with us, marry with us. The only condition: every male must be circumcised. They sell it as economic opportunity.

Will not their cattle and possessions become ours? they ask their people. The pitch lands. Every male in the city agrees, and is circumcised. The men of Shechem have been told they are gaining; they don't know they are being trapped.

Will not their cattle and possessions become ours? they ask their people. The pitch lands. Every male in the city agrees, and is circumcised. The men of Shechem have been told they are gaining; they don't know they are being trapped.

On the third day, when the men were in the worst pain of circumcision, Simeon and Levi — Dinah's full brothers — took swords, came on the city in confidence, and killed every male. They killed Hamor and Shechem and took Dinah out of his house. A massacre dressed as justice.

On the third day, when the men were in the worst pain of circumcision, Simeon and Levi — Dinah's full brothers — took swords, came on the city in confidence, and killed every male. They killed Hamor and Shechem and took Dinah out of his house. A massacre dressed as justice.

Then the other sons came upon the slain and plundered the city, because they had defiled their sister. They took all the flocks, herds, donkeys, wealth, women, and children. What had begun as outrage for Dinah ended as conquest.

Then the other sons came upon the slain and plundered the city, because they had defiled their sister. They took all the flocks, herds, donkeys, wealth, women, and children. What had begun as outrage for Dinah ended as conquest.

Then the other sons came upon the slain and plundered the city, because they had defiled their sister. They took all the flocks, herds, donkeys, wealth, women, and children. What had begun as outrage for Dinah ended as conquest.

Jacob's only word to his sons is about himself: you have troubled me, made me a stench among the people of this land. I am few in number; they will gather and destroy me and my house. He does not yet say one word about Dinah.

Should he have treated our sister like a prostitute? say the brothers. Jacob has no answer. The chapter ends without anyone speaking the right words. Dinah does not speak at all. Sometimes Scripture lets a wound stand without resolution, to make us notice.
